TECH-AGES-0001.title: Prohibit mandatory identity-based age verification
TECH-AGES-0001.stmt: Prohibit mandatory identity-based age verification systems that require government ID biometric data or persistent tracking to access lawful content

TECH-AGES-0002.title: No centralized databases
TECH-AGES-0002.stmt: Age verification systems must not create centralized databases of identity or browsing activity

TECH-AGES-0003.title: Allow privacy-preserving alternatives
TECH-AGES-0003.stmt: Allow age assurance mechanisms that preserve privacy such as local device verification zero-knowledge proofs or non-identifying credential systems

TECH-AGES-0004.title: Data minimization and deletion
TECH-AGES-0004.stmt: Any permitted age verification system must minimize data collection and prohibit retention beyond immediate verification

TECH-AGES-0005.title: Narrow scope requirements
TECH-AGES-0005.stmt: Age verification requirements must be narrowly scoped to specific high-risk content categories and may not be expanded to general internet access

TECH-AGES-0006.title: Ban surveillance proxy use
TECH-AGES-0006.stmt: Governments and private entities may not use age verification systems as a proxy for surveillance censorship or behavior tracking

TECH-AINL-0001.title: High-risk AI requires review
TECH-AINL-0001.stmt: High-risk AI systems must be subject to heightened governance review before deployment

TECH-AINL-0002.title: No automation without accountability
TECH-AINL-0002.stmt: AI systems that affect rights liberty healthcare education employment benefits policing or housing must not operate without meaningful human accountability

TECH-AINL-0003.title: Rights to notice and review
TECH-AINL-0003.stmt: No fully automated decisions in critical domains without a right to notice explanation appeal and human review

TECH-AINL-0004.title: Transparency and auditability
TECH-AINL-0004.stmt: AI systems used in public-sector decision-making must be documented transparent and independently auditable

TECH-AINL-0005.title: Liability for harms
TECH-AINL-0005.stmt: Operators and deployers of AI systems must retain legal liability for harms caused by those systems

TECH-AINL-0006.title: Testing for bias and safety
TECH-AINL-0006.stmt: AI systems must be tested for bias safety reliability and misuse risks before and after deployment

TECH-AINL-0007.title: Documentation disclosure
TECH-AINL-0007.stmt: Covered AI systems must maintain model cards documentation or equivalent public-facing disclosures appropriate to risk level

TECH-AINL-0008.title: No secret law
TECH-AINL-0008.stmt: Secret law or undisclosed AI decision systems must not be used to determine rights obligations or access to public services

TECH-ALGO-0001.title: Disclosure of algorithmic influence
TECH-ALGO-0001.stmt: People must be told when AI or algorithmic systems materially influence ranking eligibility triage moderation or access decisions

TECH-ALGO-0002.title: Right to contest decisions
TECH-ALGO-0002.stmt: People must have the right to contest materially harmful algorithmic decisions

TECH-ALGO-0003.title: Non-algorithmic alternatives
TECH-ALGO-0003.stmt: Users must have access to non-algorithmic or less personalized alternatives where practical

TECH-ALGO-0004.title: Ban manipulative optimization
TECH-ALGO-0004.stmt: Ban manipulative algorithmic optimization that targets psychological vulnerabilities for profit or political influence

TECH-ALGO-0005.title: Disclose ranking objectives
TECH-ALGO-0005.stmt: Platforms must disclose core ranking and recommendation objectives in understandable terms

TECH-ALGO-0006.title: Protected researcher access
TECH-ALGO-0006.stmt: Independent researchers must have protected access to platform data needed to study algorithmic harms subject to privacy safeguards

TECH-AUDT-0001.title: Independent auditing requirement
TECH-AUDT-0001.stmt: Covered AI systems must support independent auditing for safety bias reliability and misuse

TECH-AUDT-0002.title: Meaningful redress for harms
TECH-AUDT-0002.stmt: Affected individuals must have access to meaningful explanation and redress when harmed by AI-assisted decisions

TECH-AUDT-0003.title: Forensic logging
TECH-AUDT-0003.stmt: Organizations deploying high-risk AI must keep logs sufficient for forensic review investigation and appeals

TECH-AUDT-0004.title: Public AI registry
TECH-AUDT-0004.stmt: Government use of AI must be cataloged in a public registry except for narrowly defined classified exceptions subject to independent oversight

TECH-AUDT-0005.title: Oversight of classified systems
TECH-AUDT-0005.stmt: Classified or sensitive AI systems must still be subject to independent review under secure oversight procedures

TECH-AUTS-0001.title: Strict safeguards for high-risk systems
TECH-AUTS-0001.stmt: High-risk autonomous systems must not be deployed where failure could cause loss of liberty bodily harm or deprivation of rights without strict safeguards

TECH-AUTS-0002.title: No replacement of accountable humans
TECH-AUTS-0002.stmt: AI systems used in healthcare education benefits housing or employment decisions must not replace accountable human decision-makers

TECH-AUTS-0003.title: Prohibit automated denial systems
TECH-AUTS-0003.stmt: Automated denial systems for benefits care housing or legal status must be prohibited unless human review and appeal rights are guaranteed before harm occurs

TECH-AUTS-0004.title: Human override authority
TECH-AUTS-0004.stmt: Government agencies must maintain human override authority and documented escalation procedures for automated systems

TECH-BIOS-0001.title: Ban mass facial recognition
TECH-BIOS-0001.stmt: Ban mass facial recognition in public spaces for general law-enforcement or administrative use

TECH-BIOS-0002.title: Ban real-time crowd tracking
TECH-BIOS-0002.stmt: Ban real-time biometric tracking of crowds or demonstrations

TECH-BIOS-0003.title: No general identity infrastructure
TECH-BIOS-0003.stmt: Biometric systems may not be used as general identity infrastructure for access to lawful content or public life

TECH-BIOS-0004.title: Strict safeguards where permitted
TECH-BIOS-0004.stmt: Where biometrics are permitted they must require strict necessity proportionality auditability and deletion rules

TECH-BIOS-0005.title: Heightened data protection
TECH-BIOS-0005.stmt: Biometric data must be treated as highly sensitive protected data with stronger consent and retention rules than ordinary personal data

TECH-DATA-0001.title: Prohibit unauthorized cross-agency surveillance fusion
TECH-DATA-0001.stmt: Prohibit cross-agency fusion of surveillance datasets except where specifically authorized by statute, subject to judicial oversight, publicly disclosed in general terms, and subject to periodic reauthorization

TECH-DATA-0002.title: Ban secret dossiers
TECH-DATA-0002.stmt: Ban creation of secret AI-generated behavioral dossiers on individuals absent individualized legal cause and judicial process

TECH-DATA-0003.title: Ban government use of commercial data
TECH-DATA-0003.stmt: Ban use of commercially acquired bulk location biometric or behavioral data in AI surveillance systems

TECH-EDUS-0001.title: AI must enhance not replace learning
TECH-EDUS-0001.stmt: AI systems in education must enhance learning without replacing human instruction critical thinking or equitable access to education

TECH-EDUS-0002.title: No replacement of human educators
TECH-EDUS-0002.stmt: AI systems may not replace human educators in primary secondary or higher education contexts where direct instruction is required

TECH-EDUS-0003.title: Support not substitute
TECH-EDUS-0003.stmt: AI systems may be used to support educators and students but may not substitute for qualified teaching or mentorship

TECH-EDUS-0004.title: No sole-basis grading
TECH-EDUS-0004.stmt: AI systems may not be the sole basis for grading or evaluation in high-stakes academic decisions including advancement certification or disciplinary action

TECH-EDUS-0005.title: Right to human review
TECH-EDUS-0005.stmt: Students have the right to human review of AI-influenced grading or evaluation decisions

TECH-EDUS-0006.title: Clear policies on AI use
TECH-EDUS-0006.stmt: Educational institutions must establish clear policies on AI use that promote learning and prevent misuse without relying on unreliable detection systems

TECH-EDUS-0007.title: Evaluate for bias
TECH-EDUS-0007.stmt: AI systems used in education must be evaluated for bias and must not disadvantage students based on race gender disability socioeconomic status or language background

TECH-EDUS-0008.title: Accessibility and equity
TECH-EDUS-0008.stmt: AI tools used in education must be accessible to all students and may not create or reinforce educational inequality

TECH-EDUS-0009.title: Data minimization
TECH-EDUS-0009.stmt: Student data collected by AI systems must be limited to what is necessary for educational purposes

TECH-EDUS-0010.title: No commercial use of student data
TECH-EDUS-0010.stmt: Student data may not be sold shared or used for advertising profiling or unrelated commercial purposes

TECH-EDUS-0011.title: Enhanced protections for minors
TECH-EDUS-0011.stmt: Enhanced privacy protections are required for minors using AI systems in educational settings

TECH-EDUS-0012.title: Ban invasive surveillance
TECH-EDUS-0012.stmt: Ban use of AI systems for invasive surveillance of students including continuous monitoring behavioral tracking or biometric analysis without strict justification and safeguards

TECH-EDUS-0013.title: No emotion inference
TECH-EDUS-0013.stmt: AI systems may not be used to infer student emotions attention or psychological state for evaluation or discipline

TECH-EDUS-0014.title: No behavioral scoring
TECH-EDUS-0014.stmt: Educational institutions may not use AI systems to assign behavioral or compliance scores to students

TECH-EDUS-0015.title: Disclose AI-generated content
TECH-EDUS-0015.stmt: AI-generated educational content must be clearly identified and its limitations disclosed

TECH-EDUS-0016.title: No ideological manipulation
TECH-EDUS-0016.stmt: AI systems may not be used to impose ideological viewpoints or manipulate educational content without transparency and oversight

TECH-EDUS-0017.title: Support critical thinking
TECH-EDUS-0017.stmt: Educational use of AI must support development of critical thinking rather than passive consumption of generated answers

TECH-EDUS-0018.title: Accessibility for disabilities
TECH-EDUS-0018.stmt: AI may be used to improve accessibility for students with disabilities including translation transcription and adaptive learning tools

TECH-EDUS-0019.title: Multilingual support
TECH-EDUS-0019.stmt: AI systems should support multilingual education and reduce language barriers without compromising accuracy or fairness

TECH-EDUS-0020.title: Disclosure requirement
TECH-EDUS-0020.stmt: Educational institutions must disclose use of AI systems in teaching evaluation and administration

TECH-EDUS-0021.title: Regular independent audits
TECH-EDUS-0021.stmt: AI systems used in education must be subject to regular independent audits for bias accuracy and educational impact

TECH-EDUS-0022.title: No proprietary opacity
TECH-EDUS-0022.stmt: Vendors providing AI systems to educational institutions may not use proprietary claims to avoid oversight or accountability

TECH-EDUS-0023.title: Demonstrate educational benefit
TECH-EDUS-0023.stmt: AI systems must demonstrate educational benefit through evidence before widespread deployment in classrooms

TECH-EDUS-0024.title: Informed consent for testing
TECH-EDUS-0024.stmt: Students may not be used as unwitting test subjects for AI systems without informed consent and appropriate safeguards

TECH-ENVS-0001.title: Sustainable operations required
TECH-ENVS-0001.stmt: AI systems and infrastructure must not externalize environmental costs and must operate within sustainable limits for energy water materials and ecological impact

TECH-ENVS-0002.title: Carbon neutrality requirement
TECH-ENVS-0002.stmt: Large-scale AI systems and data centers must meet strict carbon neutrality or carbon-negative requirements over their full lifecycle

TECH-ENVS-0003.title: Energy disclosure
TECH-ENVS-0003.stmt: Operators of large AI systems must publicly disclose energy usage sources emissions and efficiency metrics

TECH-ENVS-0004.title: Renewable energy supply
TECH-ENVS-0004.stmt: High-consumption AI infrastructure must supply or offset its energy usage through dedicated renewable or low-impact sources rather than relying on general grid extraction

TECH-ENVS-0005.title: Water usage disclosure
TECH-ENVS-0005.stmt: AI infrastructure operators must disclose water usage including cooling consumption and local environmental impact

TECH-ENVS-0006.title: Water resource protection
TECH-ENVS-0006.stmt: AI systems must not disproportionately strain local water resources particularly in drought-prone or vulnerable regions

TECH-ENVS-0007.title: Materials sourcing standards
TECH-ENVS-0007.stmt: Materials used in AI hardware including rare earth elements must be sourced under strict environmental and labor standards

TECH-ENVS-0008.title: Lifecycle responsibility
TECH-ENVS-0008.stmt: AI hardware producers must be responsible for full lifecycle impacts including manufacturing durability and end-of-life disposal

TECH-ENVS-0009.title: Durability and repairability
TECH-ENVS-0009.stmt: AI-related hardware must meet durability repairability and reuse standards to reduce electronic waste

TECH-ENVS-0010.title: Recycling programs
TECH-ENVS-0010.stmt: Operators must implement responsible recycling and disposal programs for AI hardware and infrastructure components

TECH-ENVS-0011.title: Internalize environmental costs
TECH-ENVS-0011.stmt: Companies developing or deploying AI systems must internalize environmental costs rather than shifting them to the public or ecosystems

TECH-ENVS-0012.title: Environmental impact assessments
TECH-ENVS-0012.stmt: Large-scale AI deployments must undergo environmental impact assessments prior to construction or expansion

TECH-ENVS-0013.title: Support climate efforts
TECH-ENVS-0013.stmt: AI should be used to support climate modeling environmental monitoring and mitigation efforts under transparent and accountable frameworks

TECH-ENVS-0014.title: Resource optimization
TECH-ENVS-0014.stmt: AI may be used to optimize energy water and resource usage provided it does not create hidden environmental tradeoffs or inequitable impacts

TECH-ENVS-0015.title: Grid modernization support
TECH-ENVS-0015.stmt: AI systems may support modernization of electrical grids microgrids and renewable energy systems while maintaining transparency and public accountability

TECH-ENVS-0016.title: No misrepresentation
TECH-ENVS-0016.stmt: AI companies may not misrepresent environmental impact through selective reporting offsets or unverifiable sustainability claims

TECH-ENVS-0017.title: Standardized reporting
TECH-ENVS-0017.stmt: Environmental reporting for AI systems must follow standardized verifiable metrics to prevent manipulation or selective disclosure

TECH-ENVS-0018.title: Environmental justice protection
TECH-ENVS-0018.stmt: AI infrastructure may not disproportionately locate environmental burdens in disadvantaged communities or regions

TECH-ENVS-0019.title: No global offloading
TECH-ENVS-0019.stmt: Environmental costs of AI supply chains must not be offloaded to developing regions without equivalent protections and standards

TECH-ENVS-0020.title: Integration with national policy
TECH-ENVS-0020.stmt: AI infrastructure policy must integrate with national energy grid modernization renewable investment and public infrastructure strategies

TECH-ENVS-0021.title: Noise pollution standards
TECH-ENVS-0021.stmt: Establish standards for noise pollution from AI data centers and other large data centers including monitoring mitigation and local community protections

TECH-ENVS-0022.title: Water consumption caps
TECH-ENVS-0022.stmt: Large-scale AI data centers must operate within binding water consumption limits based on regional availability and must report actual usage against permitted caps on a publicly accessible basis

TECH-ENVS-0023.title: Water-stressed region restrictions
TECH-ENVS-0023.stmt: AI infrastructure requiring significant water cooling may not be sited in regions classified as water-stressed or drought-prone under federal or state water scarcity designations without demonstrated water-neutral mitigation plans and public review

TECH-ENVS-0024.title: Groundwater and aquifer protection
TECH-ENVS-0024.stmt: AI data centers may not draw on groundwater or aquifer sources in ways that degrade water tables reduce flows to downstream users or damage ecosystems without full environmental review, public notice, and binding mitigation commitments

TECH-ENVS-0025.title: Water recycling and reclamation
TECH-ENVS-0025.stmt: New large-scale AI data centers must implement water recycling, reclamation, or closed-loop cooling systems to the maximum extent technically feasible and must report recycling rates alongside gross consumption figures

TECH-ENVS-0026.title: Drought contingency requirements
TECH-ENVS-0026.stmt: Operators of high-water-consumption AI infrastructure must maintain and publicly file drought contingency plans specifying operational curtailment thresholds during declared water emergencies, priority for municipal and agricultural water users, and timelines for transitioning to low-water or air-cooled alternatives

TECH-ENVS-0027.title: Community water impact review
TECH-ENVS-0027.stmt: Before approving large data center water permits, regulators must conduct a community water impact review assessing effects on residential water access, municipal supply systems, agricultural users, and environmental flows, with mandatory public comment periods and binding mitigation requirements

TECH-FINC-0001.title: Fairness and transparency in finance
TECH-FINC-0001.stmt: AI systems in finance credit and insurance must not undermine fairness transparency equal access or protection from discrimination in essential economic systems

TECH-FINC-0002.title: No automated denial of credit
TECH-FINC-0002.stmt: AI systems may not independently deny credit loans mortgages refinancing or other essential financial services without direct human review and accountability

TECH-FINC-0003.title: Human review before denial
TECH-FINC-0003.stmt: Any decision that would deny restrict or materially worsen access to credit or lending must be made directly and independently by a qualified human reviewer before harm occurs

TECH-FINC-0004.title: No opaque criteria
TECH-FINC-0004.stmt: AI systems used in underwriting or creditworthiness assessments may not rely on opaque or unexplainable criteria that materially affect outcomes

TECH-FINC-0005.title: Anti-discrimination requirement
TECH-FINC-0005.stmt: AI systems in finance and insurance must not discriminate based on protected characteristics or proxies for those characteristics including zip code language behavior or network data

TECH-FINC-0006.title: Regular audits for bias
TECH-FINC-0006.stmt: AI systems used in lending underwriting pricing or claims decisions must be regularly audited for disparate impact and discriminatory outcomes

TECH-FINC-0007.title: No automated insurance denial
TECH-FINC-0007.stmt: AI systems may not independently deny restrict reduce or terminate insurance coverage or claims without direct and independent human review

TECH-FINC-0008.title: Independent human judgment required
TECH-FINC-0008.stmt: Human reviewers in insurance decisions may not rely solely on AI-generated recommendations and must exercise independent judgment

TECH-FINC-0009.title: AI for approval not denial
TECH-FINC-0009.stmt: AI systems may be used to assist or expedite approval of insurance claims or coverage but may not be used as the primary basis for denial restriction or reduction

TECH-FINC-0010.title: No negative inference from AI
TECH-FINC-0010.stmt: Absence of AI approval or recommendation may not be used as evidence or implicit justification for denial restriction or delay of insurance claims or coverage

TECH-FINC-0011.title: No behavioral surveillance scoring
TECH-FINC-0011.stmt: AI systems may not use generalized behavioral surveillance or social scoring to determine eligibility pricing or access in finance credit or insurance

TECH-FINC-0012.title: Transparent risk scoring
TECH-FINC-0012.stmt: Risk scoring in finance and insurance must be based on transparent lawful and relevant factors and may not rely on opaque behavioral inference

TECH-FINC-0013.title: No vulnerability profiling
TECH-FINC-0013.stmt: AI systems may not use individualized vulnerability profiling to impose exploitative pricing rates fees or terms

TECH-FINC-0014.title: Fair housing access
TECH-FINC-0014.stmt: AI systems used in mortgage housing finance or rental screening must not undermine fair access to housing or reproduce historical discrimination

TECH-FINC-0015.title: Tenant screening limits
TECH-FINC-0015.stmt: Tenant screening and housing-related AI systems may not rely on opaque scoring or unverifiable data that materially affect housing access

TECH-FINC-0016.title: Right to explanation
TECH-FINC-0016.stmt: People have the right to a meaningful explanation of AI-influenced financial credit or insurance decisions affecting them

TECH-FINC-0017.title: Right to appeal
TECH-FINC-0017.stmt: People must have access to a timely human appeal process for materially adverse AI-influenced financial credit or insurance decisions

TECH-FINC-0018.title: Disclosure requirement
TECH-FINC-0018.stmt: Entities using AI in consequential financial or insurance decisions must disclose when such systems materially influence outcomes

TECH-FINC-0019.title: Data minimization
TECH-FINC-0019.stmt: AI systems in finance and insurance may collect only data that is strictly necessary for lawful and relevant decision-making

TECH-FINC-0020.title: No data sales or repurposing
TECH-FINC-0020.stmt: Financial and insurance data used by AI systems may not be sold shared or repurposed for unrelated commercial targeting or behavioral profiling

TECH-FINC-0021.title: No secret profile enrichment
TECH-FINC-0021.stmt: Entities may not secretly enrich financial or insurance profiles with third-party consumer data social graph data or surveillance-derived data without explicit lawful authority and disclosure

TECH-FINC-0022.title: No cross-system denial loops
TECH-FINC-0022.stmt: AI systems may not be used to create cross-system denial loops where credit insurance housing or public-benefit decisions reinforce each other without independent review and due process

TECH-FINC-0023.title: Independent audits
TECH-FINC-0023.stmt: AI systems used in finance credit and insurance must be subject to regular independent audits for bias fairness legality and consumer harm

TECH-FINC-0024.title: No proprietary opacity
TECH-FINC-0024.stmt: Trade secrecy or proprietary claims may not be used to prevent meaningful oversight explanation or appeal in consequential financial or insurance AI systems

TECH-FINC-0025.title: Documentation requirement
TECH-FINC-0025.stmt: Entities deploying consequential AI systems in finance and insurance must maintain documentation sufficient for review audit and accountability

TECH-FINC-0026.title: No exploitation of vulnerability
TECH-FINC-0026.stmt: AI systems may not be used to identify and exploit financially vulnerable individuals through targeted loans fees insurance terms or sales practices

TECH-FINC-0027.title: No impersonation
TECH-FINC-0027.stmt: AI systems in finance and insurance may not impersonate human advisors or misrepresent their authority capabilities or obligations to consumers

TECH-FINC-0028.title: Public-interest standards for essential systems
TECH-FINC-0028.stmt: Where finance credit or insurance systems function as essential gateways to housing healthcare transportation or economic participation they must be governed by heightened public-interest standards

TECH-FINC-0029.title: No automatic exclusion from essential systems
TECH-FINC-0029.stmt: AI systems must not be allowed to automatically exclude people from essential economic systems needed for basic social participation

TECH-GOVN-0001.title: Preserve due process and rights
TECH-GOVN-0001.stmt: AI systems used by government or public-service entities must not undermine due process equal protection transparency accountability or access to rights and services

TECH-GOVN-0002.title: No automated denial of benefits
TECH-GOVN-0002.stmt: AI systems may not independently deny terminate reduce or suspend access to public benefits services or legal status determinations

TECH-GOVN-0003.title: Human decision-maker before harm
TECH-GOVN-0003.stmt: Any decision that would deny reduce terminate or delay benefits services or rights must be made directly and independently by a qualified human decision-maker before harm occurs

TECH-GOVN-0004.title: Independent human judgment required
TECH-GOVN-0004.stmt: Human reviewers may not rely solely on AI-generated recommendations when making public-service or benefits decisions and must exercise independent judgment

TECH-GOVN-0005.title: AI for approval not denial
TECH-GOVN-0005.stmt: AI systems may be used to help identify likely eligibility or expedite approvals but may not be used as the primary basis for denial reduction or termination of benefits or services

TECH-GOVN-0006.title: Right to explanation
TECH-GOVN-0006.stmt: Individuals have the right to a meaningful explanation of any AI-influenced government decision affecting benefits services legal status or access to rights

TECH-GOVN-0007.title: Right to appeal
TECH-GOVN-0007.stmt: Individuals must have a timely accessible appeal process before a human decision-maker for any materially adverse AI-influenced government decision

TECH-GOVN-0008.title: Disclosure requirement
TECH-GOVN-0008.stmt: Government agencies must clearly disclose when AI systems materially influence decisions affecting the public

TECH-GOVN-0009.title: No behavioral scoring
TECH-GOVN-0009.stmt: Government may not use AI systems to assign generalized risk trustworthiness fraud or compliance scores to individuals for access to public services or rights

TECH-GOVN-0010.title: No discriminatory profiling
TECH-GOVN-0010.stmt: Government AI systems may not use protected characteristics or their proxies to infer eligibility suspicion dangerousness or worthiness

TECH-GOVN-0011.title: No social conformity scoring
TECH-GOVN-0011.stmt: Government may not use AI systems to monitor or score behavioral conformity ideological alignment or social desirability as a condition of access to services or benefits

TECH-GOVN-0012.title: Access to human representatives
TECH-GOVN-0012.stmt: People must retain access to human government representatives for matters involving benefits legal status healthcare housing education or essential public services

TECH-GOVN-0013.title: No forced AI-only channels
TECH-GOVN-0013.stmt: Government may not force individuals into AI-only service channels where lack of human access would impair fairness comprehension or ability to contest decisions

TECH-GOVN-0014.title: Accessibility for vulnerable populations
TECH-GOVN-0014.stmt: AI-enabled public services must be accessible to disabled low-income elderly and limited-English-proficiency users and may not create new access barriers

TECH-GOVN-0015.title: No automated disability denials
TECH-GOVN-0015.stmt: AI systems may not be used to deny disability benefits or override licensed medical evidence without transparent human review and due process

TECH-GOVN-0016.title: No mass fraud sweeps
TECH-GOVN-0016.stmt: Government may not use AI systems to conduct mass fraud sweeps or suspicion-based benefits enforcement without individualized legal standards and human review

TECH-GOVN-0017.title: No automated termination of essential benefits
TECH-GOVN-0017.stmt: AI systems may not automatically terminate housing food healthcare disability or income-support benefits based on scoring anomalies or probabilistic inference

TECH-GOVN-0018.title: No automated immigration decisions
TECH-GOVN-0018.stmt: AI systems may not independently determine immigration status detention deportation asylum credibility or family reunification outcomes

TECH-GOVN-0019.title: No credibility inference in immigration
TECH-GOVN-0019.stmt: Government may not use AI systems to infer truthfulness credibility intent or dangerousness in immigration or asylum contexts

TECH-GOVN-0020.title: Human review for immigration decisions
TECH-GOVN-0020.stmt: Any AI-influenced immigration or detention decision must be reviewed and decided by a human official subject to due process and judicial oversight

TECH-GOVN-0021.title: Vendor accountability
TECH-GOVN-0021.stmt: Private vendors and contractors supplying AI systems to government are subject to the same legal constitutional and ethical constraints as the government entities using them

TECH-GOVN-0022.title: No proprietary opacity
TECH-GOVN-0022.stmt: Trade secrecy or proprietary claims may not be used to prevent meaningful oversight explanation or appeal for AI systems used in public decision-making

TECH-GOVN-0023.title: Procurement disclosure
TECH-GOVN-0023.stmt: Government procurement of AI systems must include public disclosure of purpose capabilities limitations oversight structure and vendor conflicts of interest

TECH-GOVN-0024.title: Regular independent audits
TECH-GOVN-0024.stmt: Government AI systems must undergo regular independent audits for legality accuracy bias rights impacts and administrative fairness

TECH-GOVN-0025.title: Public AI registry
TECH-GOVN-0025.stmt: All materially consequential government AI systems must be listed in a public registry with clear descriptions of use authority and oversight

TECH-GOVN-0026.title: Sunset and reauthorization
TECH-GOVN-0026.stmt: Government AI systems affecting rights benefits or legal status must include sunset dates and periodic reauthorization requirements

TECH-GOVN-0027.title: Explicit legal authority required
TECH-GOVN-0027.stmt: Government agencies may not deploy consequential AI systems without explicit legal authority and defined limits on scope and use

TECH-GOVN-0028.title: No unwitting test subjects
TECH-GOVN-0028.stmt: Government may not use the public as unwitting test subjects for consequential AI systems without lawful authority transparency and ethical safeguards

TECH-GOVN-0029.title: Right to challenge system legality
TECH-GOVN-0029.stmt: Individuals must be able to challenge not only a government AI decision but also the legality and validity of the system used to make it

TECH-IMMS-0001.title: Ban opaque immigration AI
TECH-IMMS-0001.stmt: Ban use of opaque or unreviewable AI systems in immigration enforcement detention or deportation decisions

TECH-IMMS-0002.title: Ban risk scoring for detention
TECH-IMMS-0002.stmt: Ban AI risk scoring systems used to justify immigration detention denial of release or family separation

TECH-INTL-0004.title: Common carrier treatment
TECH-INTL-0004.stmt: Internet service providers and core digital network infrastructure must be treated as common carriers or equivalent neutral service providers

TECH-INTL-0005.title: Non-discrimination requirement
TECH-INTL-0005.stmt: Common carrier obligations must prohibit discrimination based on content source destination or political or competitive considerations

TECH-INTL-0006.title: No blocking or throttling
TECH-INTL-0006.stmt: Network operators may not prioritize degrade or block traffic for competitive advantage political influence or economic coercion

TECH-INTL-0007.title: Narrow technical exceptions
TECH-INTL-0007.stmt: Net neutrality frameworks must allow narrowly scoped exceptions for technical optimization security reliability and research and development

TECH-INTL-0008.title: Transparent exceptions
TECH-INTL-0008.stmt: All exceptions to neutrality must be transparent auditable and prohibited from conferring unfair market or political advantage

TECH-INTL-0009.title: Protection from rollback
TECH-INTL-0009.stmt: Core neutrality and access protections must be insulated from administrative rollback through regulatory or executive action alone

TECH-JUDS-0001.title: No AI sentencing
TECH-JUDS-0001.stmt: AI systems may not be used to determine sentencing bail or punishment in criminal justice proceedings

TECH-JUDS-0002.title: No risk scoring
TECH-JUDS-0002.stmt: AI systems may not assign risk scores for recidivism dangerousness or likelihood of compliance in judicial decision-making

TECH-JUDS-0003.title: No jury profiling
TECH-JUDS-0003.stmt: AI systems may not be used to profile influence or select jurors based on behavioral psychological or demographic inference

TECH-JUDS-0004.title: Strict admissibility standards
TECH-JUDS-0004.stmt: AI-generated or AI-analyzed evidence must meet strict standards for reliability transparency and cross-examination before being admissible in court

TECH-JUDS-0005.title: Right to examine AI evidence
TECH-JUDS-0005.stmt: Defendants and parties must have the right to examine challenge and obtain disclosure of AI systems used in evidentiary processes

TECH-JUDS-0006.title: AI for wrongful conviction review
TECH-JUDS-0006.stmt: AI may be used to assist in identifying wrongful convictions inconsistencies or procedural errors and to accelerate appeals and case reviews

TECH-JUDS-0007.title: Identify systemic bias
TECH-JUDS-0007.stmt: AI should be used to identify systemic bias in sentencing policing and prosecution patterns to support corrective action

TECH-JUDS-0008.title: No AI final determinations
TECH-JUDS-0008.stmt: Judges and court staff may not rely on AI systems to make final determinations of fact law credibility or case outcome

TECH-JUDS-0009.title: Clerical use only with disclosure
TECH-JUDS-0009.stmt: AI may be used to assist with clerical summarization research and document organization only under clear disclosure and human verification requirements

TECH-JUDS-0010.title: Disclose material AI use
TECH-JUDS-0010.stmt: Courts must disclose material use of AI in opinion drafting legal analysis record summarization or case management where it could affect litigants' rights or case outcomes

TECH-JUDS-0011.title: No unfair docket prioritization
TECH-JUDS-0011.stmt: AI systems may not be used to prioritize dockets motions or hearings in ways that create unfairness bias or unequal access to timely justice

TECH-JUDS-0012.title: Preserve accountable reasoning
TECH-JUDS-0012.stmt: Any AI-assisted judicial workflow must preserve a human-authored accountable and reviewable chain of reasoning for consequential rulings

TECH-JUDS-0013.title: Evidence authentication
TECH-JUDS-0013.stmt: AI-generated or AI-enhanced evidence must be authenticated with documented provenance methodology and chain of custody before admissibility

TECH-JUDS-0014.title: Technical disclosure for challenge
TECH-JUDS-0014.stmt: Parties must receive sufficient technical disclosure to challenge the reliability validity and limitations of AI-generated or AI-analyzed evidence

TECH-JUDS-0015.title: Scientific validation required
TECH-JUDS-0015.stmt: Courts may not admit AI outputs as expert-like evidence unless their methods are scientifically valid independently testable and subject to adversarial challenge

TECH-JUDS-0016.title: Synthetic media high-risk
TECH-JUDS-0016.stmt: Synthetic media evidence must be presumptively treated as high-risk and require enhanced authentication standards before admission

TECH-JUDS-0017.title: Preserve analysis logs
TECH-JUDS-0017.stmt: If AI is used to analyze evidence all material logs model assumptions and transformation steps relevant to the output must be preservable and reviewable

TECH-JUDS-0018.title: Defense access to AI disclosures
TECH-JUDS-0018.stmt: If prosecutors or the state use AI systems in investigation charging discovery or litigation support defendants must have equal access to the relevant outputs disclosures and technical challenge mechanisms

TECH-JUDS-0019.title: Defense funding for AI parity
TECH-JUDS-0019.stmt: Public defenders and defense counsel must be provided funding and access sufficient to avoid AI-driven asymmetry between prosecution and defense

TECH-JUDS-0020.title: No AI-driven plea pressure
TECH-JUDS-0020.stmt: AI systems may not be used to pressure plea deals through opaque case scoring predicted conviction metrics or punishment leverage models

TECH-JUDS-0021.title: Guard against prosecutorial AI advantage
TECH-JUDS-0021.stmt: Courts must guard against prosecutorial advantage created by proprietary or undisclosed AI tools unavailable to defense

TECH-JUDS-0022.title: No juror profiling
TECH-JUDS-0022.stmt: AI systems may not be used to profile rank influence or strategically shape juror selection based on behavioral psychological or demographic inference

TECH-JUDS-0023.title: AI reconstructions must be disclosed
TECH-JUDS-0023.stmt: AI-generated reconstructions simulations or visualizations shown to juries must be clearly disclosed as generated demonstrative material and subject to strict admissibility standards

TECH-JUDS-0024.title: No AI summaries to juries
TECH-JUDS-0024.stmt: Courts may not provide juries with AI-generated summaries interpretations or credibility assessments of testimony or evidence

TECH-JUDS-0025.title: No distortion of evidentiary record
TECH-JUDS-0025.stmt: Jury-facing use of AI must not distort the evidentiary record or create false impressions of certainty precision or neutrality

TECH-JUDS-0026.title: Default to prohibition
TECH-JUDS-0026.stmt: AI use in courts and legal proceedings must default to prohibition except where narrowly permitted for transparent auditable non-generative analytical purposes under strict human oversight

TECH-JUDS-0027.title: Ban AI-generated evidence
TECH-JUDS-0027.stmt: AI-generated or AI-fabricated evidence including images video audio text or reconstructions is not admissible in legal proceedings

TECH-JUDS-0028.title: Ban AI-enhanced evidence
TECH-JUDS-0028.stmt: AI-enhanced evidence that alters interpretation content or meaning of underlying evidence is not admissible

TECH-JUDS-0029.title: Analytical use only
TECH-JUDS-0029.stmt: AI may be used only for analytical purposes on existing evidence where it does not generate new content and does not alter underlying data

TECH-JUDS-0030.title: No generative AI for evidence
TECH-JUDS-0030.stmt: Generative AI systems including large language models may not be used for evidentiary analysis in legal proceedings

TECH-JUDS-0031.title: Verifiable methods only
TECH-JUDS-0031.stmt: Permitted analytical systems must use verifiable reproducible methods such as statistical or traditional machine learning models rather than generative systems

TECH-JUDS-0032.title: Disclose analytical AI use
TECH-JUDS-0032.stmt: Any use of AI in evidence analysis must be explicitly disclosed including methods data inputs limitations and error rates

TECH-JUDS-0033.title: No black-box systems
TECH-JUDS-0033.stmt: Black-box AI systems that cannot be meaningfully explained tested or challenged are not admissible in evidentiary contexts

TECH-JUDS-0034.title: AI not expert witnesses
TECH-JUDS-0034.stmt: AI systems may not be presented as expert witnesses or as authoritative sources in legal proceedings

TECH-JUDS-0035.title: Human experts accountable
TECH-JUDS-0035.stmt: All expert testimony must be provided by qualified human experts who are accountable for their conclusions

TECH-JUDS-0036.title: Disclose AI-assisted expert analysis
TECH-JUDS-0036.stmt: Human experts using AI-assisted analysis must disclose such use and remain fully responsible for all conclusions

TECH-JUDS-0037.title: No AI-drafted opinions
TECH-JUDS-0037.stmt: Judges and justices may not use generative AI systems to draft write or materially shape judicial opinions or rulings

TECH-JUDS-0038.title: No AI legal reasoning
TECH-JUDS-0038.stmt: AI systems may not substitute for judicial reasoning legal analysis or interpretation of law

TECH-JUDS-0039.title: Limited clerical use
TECH-JUDS-0039.stmt: AI may be used for limited clerical tasks such as document organization or formatting only when it does not affect legal reasoning or outcomes

TECH-JUDS-0040.title: No AI reconstructions to juries
TECH-JUDS-0040.stmt: AI-generated reconstructions simulations or demonstrative materials may not be shown to juries

TECH-JUDS-0041.title: No AI summaries of evidence to juries
TECH-JUDS-0041.stmt: AI systems may not be used to summarize interpret or present evidence or testimony to juries

TECH-JUDS-0042.title: No AI influence on jurors
TECH-JUDS-0042.stmt: AI systems may not be used to influence juror perception behavior or decision-making directly or indirectly

TECH-JUDS-0043.title: Recognize AI risks
TECH-JUDS-0043.stmt: Courts must recognize that AI systems can amplify confirmation bias hallucination and false certainty and must treat AI outputs as inherently high-risk

TECH-JUDS-0044.title: Elevated scrutiny standards
TECH-JUDS-0044.stmt: Any permitted AI-assisted analysis must meet elevated standards of reliability scrutiny and adversarial testing beyond traditional evidence

TECH-JUDS-0045.title: Right to challenge AI analysis
TECH-JUDS-0045.stmt: All parties must have full rights to challenge any AI-assisted analysis including methodology assumptions and outputs

TECH-JUDS-0046.title: Reproducibility requirement
TECH-JUDS-0046.stmt: AI-assisted analysis must be reproducible by opposing parties using the same inputs and methods

TECH-JUDS-0047.title: Preserve analysis logs
TECH-JUDS-0047.stmt: All AI-assisted evidentiary analysis must include preserved logs of inputs outputs and transformation steps

TECH-JUDS-0048.title: Audit access requirement
TECH-JUDS-0048.stmt: Courts and opposing parties must have access to all materials necessary to audit AI-assisted analysis

TECH-JUDS-0049.title: No AI family court determinations
TECH-JUDS-0049.stmt: AI systems may not be used to determine custody visitation parental fitness or family placement outcomes without direct accountable human judicial decision-making

TECH-JUDS-0050.title: No parental fitness inference
TECH-JUDS-0050.stmt: AI systems may not infer parental fitness abuse risk credibility or child welfare outcomes from behavioral proxies psychological profiling or opaque risk models

TECH-JUDS-0051.title: No AI in family court recommendations
TECH-JUDS-0051.stmt: Family courts may not rely on AI-generated summaries recommendations or credibility assessments in matters involving custody abuse allegations or child welfare

TECH-JUDS-0052.title: Disclose family court AI use
TECH-JUDS-0052.stmt: Any AI-assisted tool used in family court administration must be fully disclosed and may not materially affect outcomes without adversarial challenge rights

TECH-JUDS-0053.title: No automated evictions
TECH-JUDS-0053.stmt: AI systems may not be used to automate or materially drive eviction outcomes without direct human judicial review and full due process protections

TECH-JUDS-0054.title: No tenant risk scoring
TECH-JUDS-0054.stmt: Courts may not rely on AI-generated tenant risk scores rental-behavior predictions or opaque housing analytics in eviction or housing-access proceedings

TECH-JUDS-0055.title: Disclose housing AI use
TECH-JUDS-0055.stmt: Landlords and housing litigants using AI-assisted evidence or analytics in court must disclose such use and provide sufficient information for challenge and review

TECH-JUDS-0056.title: No eviction acceleration through AI
TECH-JUDS-0056.stmt: Housing courts must not use AI systems to accelerate case throughput in ways that reduce meaningful notice hearing access or tenant defense rights

TECH-JUDS-0057.title: No AI in administrative adjudication
TECH-JUDS-0057.stmt: AI systems may not independently determine outcomes in administrative hearings involving benefits licensing immigration disability housing or employment rights

TECH-JUDS-0058.title: No opaque scoring in administrative hearings
TECH-JUDS-0058.stmt: Administrative adjudication may not rely on opaque AI scoring or recommendation systems to determine credibility eligibility compliance or sanction outcomes

TECH-JUDS-0059.title: Disclose AI in administrative proceedings
TECH-JUDS-0059.stmt: Parties in administrative proceedings have the right to know when AI materially influenced a recommendation record summary or proposed outcome

TECH-JUDS-0060.title: Administrative appeal rights
TECH-JUDS-0060.stmt: Administrative agencies must provide meaningful human review and appeal for any AI-influenced adverse decision

TECH-JUDS-0061.title: No AI probation/parole decisions
TECH-JUDS-0061.stmt: AI systems may not be used to determine probation parole revocation supervision intensity or conditions of release through opaque risk scoring

TECH-JUDS-0062.title: No proxy-based supervision scoring
TECH-JUDS-0062.stmt: AI systems may not assign supervision risk scores based on proxies for race class disability geography or protected activity

TECH-JUDS-0063.title: Individualized human review required
TECH-JUDS-0063.stmt: Probation and parole decisions must be based on individualized human review and may not be automated through behavioral prediction systems

TECH-JUDS-0064.title: AI to identify bias in probation
TECH-JUDS-0064.stmt: AI may be used to identify patterns of bias inconsistency or unlawful disparity in probation and parole systems under transparent oversight

TECH-JUDS-0065.title: No AI fines and fees escalation
TECH-JUDS-0065.stmt: AI systems may not be used to escalate fines fees collections or penalties based on predictive payment scoring or behavioral profiling

TECH-JUDS-0066.title: No payment coercion through AI
TECH-JUDS-0066.stmt: Courts and governments may not use AI to pressure payment compliance through opaque threat scoring or automated penalty escalation

TECH-JUDS-0067.title: No AI debt-to-punishment escalation
TECH-JUDS-0067.stmt: AI systems may not be used to convert administrative debt or missed payments into harsher legal consequences without direct human review and due process

TECH-JUDS-0068.title: AI to identify predatory fine patterns
TECH-JUDS-0068.stmt: AI may be used to identify unlawful disparities or predatory fine-and-fee patterns for corrective review under public oversight

TECH-JUDS-0069.title: AI for legal aid access
TECH-JUDS-0069.stmt: AI may be used to assist legal aid intake document navigation and access to information provided it does not replace licensed legal counsel where counsel is required or appropriate

TECH-JUDS-0070.title: Disclose legal-aid AI limits
TECH-JUDS-0070.stmt: AI tools used in legal-aid contexts must clearly disclose their limits and may not present themselves as human attorneys or as a substitute for legal representation

TECH-JUDS-0071.title: Public legal-aid tools must be fair
TECH-JUDS-0071.stmt: Public legal-aid AI tools must be designed for accessibility clarity and fairness and may not steer users away from asserting rights or seeking counsel

TECH-JUDS-0072.title: Fund public legal access tools
TECH-JUDS-0072.stmt: Governments and courts should fund public-interest legal access tools that improve navigation and review without automating final legal judgments

TECH-JUDS-0073.title: AI records systems must be auditable
TECH-JUDS-0073.stmt: AI systems used to classify search summarize or prioritize court records must be auditable and may not distort case visibility or public access

TECH-JUDS-0074.title: No unequal filing access
TECH-JUDS-0074.stmt: Court administrative AI systems may not create unequal access to filing scheduling or record retrieval based on hidden prioritization logic

TECH-JUDS-0075.title: Preserve record accuracy
TECH-JUDS-0075.stmt: Public court records systems using AI must preserve accuracy transparency and the ability to correct errors promptly

TECH-JUDS-0076.title: Human override for court admin AI
TECH-JUDS-0076.stmt: Courts must maintain human override and review mechanisms for all AI-assisted administrative systems affecting litigants or public access

TECH-JUDS-0077.title: AI limited to assistive functions
TECH-JUDS-0077.stmt: Where AI is permitted in judicial contexts it must be limited to transparent non-generative assistive functions and may not determine rights liabilities punishment credibility or case outcome

TECH-JUDS-0078.title: No credibility assessment
TECH-JUDS-0078.stmt: AI systems may not be used to assess credibility truthfulness deception or intent of witnesses defendants or parties in legal proceedings

TECH-JUDS-0079.title: No behavioral inference in court
TECH-JUDS-0079.stmt: Behavioral biometric or speech-analysis AI tools may not be used to infer emotional state truthfulness or reliability in courtroom contexts

TECH-JUDS-0080.title: No AI alteration of police footage
TECH-JUDS-0080.stmt: AI systems may not be used to reinterpret enhance or selectively analyze police body-camera or surveillance footage in ways that alter evidentiary meaning

TECH-JUDS-0081.title: Preserve full context in evidence
TECH-JUDS-0081.stmt: AI-assisted analysis of law-enforcement evidence must preserve full context and may not selectively highlight or suppress information to support a narrative

TECH-JUDS-0082.title: No sole-basis AI translation
TECH-JUDS-0082.stmt: AI translation or interpretation systems may not be used as the sole basis for legal understanding in court proceedings where rights or outcomes are affected

TECH-JUDS-0083.title: Human interpreters required
TECH-JUDS-0083.stmt: Human-certified interpreters are required for critical proceedings and AI translation may only be used as a supplementary tool with disclosure

TECH-JUDS-0084.title: Forensic AI validation required
TECH-JUDS-0084.stmt: AI systems used in forensic laboratories must meet strict scientific validation standards and may not operate as opaque or unreviewable systems

TECH-JUDS-0085.title: Test forensic AI systems
TECH-JUDS-0085.stmt: Forensic AI systems must be independently tested for accuracy bias and reproducibility before use in any evidentiary pipeline

TECH-JUDS-0086.title: No AI reconstruction of sealed records
TECH-JUDS-0086.stmt: AI systems may not reconstruct infer or surface sealed expunged or legally protected records through data aggregation or inference

TECH-JUDS-0087.title: Enforce record protections
TECH-JUDS-0087.stmt: Use of AI to bypass legal protections on records privacy or expungement is prohibited and subject to enforcement

TECH-JUDS-0088.title: No AI influence campaigns on juries
TECH-JUDS-0088.stmt: AI-generated content may not be used to influence jurors witnesses or proceedings outside the courtroom through targeted media or messaging

TECH-JUDS-0089.title: Mitigate public AI influence on trials
TECH-JUDS-0089.stmt: Courts must recognize and mitigate risks of AI-driven public influence campaigns that could affect jury impartiality or trial fairness

TECH-JUDS-0090.title: AI version control required
TECH-JUDS-0090.stmt: AI systems used in any permitted judicial context must have version control and may not change behavior during active cases without disclosure and review

TECH-JUDS-0091.title: Revalidate AI changes
TECH-JUDS-0091.stmt: Material changes to AI systems used in legal contexts must trigger revalidation and may not retroactively affect prior analyses without disclosure

TECH-JUDS-0092.title: No shift of burden or standards
TECH-JUDS-0092.stmt: Use of AI systems may not shift burden of proof evidentiary standards or procedural rights of any party in legal proceedings

TECH-JUDS-0093.title: Transparent AI procurement
TECH-JUDS-0093.stmt: Courts and judicial systems must use transparent procurement processes for any AI system including public disclosure and review before deployment

TECH-JUDS-0094.title: Independent validation before deployment
TECH-JUDS-0094.stmt: AI systems may not be deployed in judicial contexts without independent validation for fairness reliability and legal compliance

TECH-JUDS-0095.title: Authority to suspend AI systems
TECH-JUDS-0095.stmt: Courts must maintain the authority to suspend or prohibit use of any AI system that demonstrates bias unreliability or legal risk

TECH-JUDS-0096.title: Party right to request suspension
TECH-JUDS-0096.stmt: Parties must have the right to request suspension of AI system use in a case where fairness or reliability is in question

TECH-LABS-0001.title: Protect worker rights and dignity
TECH-LABS-0001.stmt: AI systems in employment must not undermine worker rights dignity privacy or fair access to economic opportunity

TECH-LABS-0002.title: No automated employment decisions
TECH-LABS-0002.stmt: AI systems may not make fully automated hiring firing promotion or compensation decisions without meaningful human review and accountability

TECH-LABS-0003.title: No opaque candidate filtering
TECH-LABS-0003.stmt: AI systems may not be used to filter or rank job candidates using opaque or unexplainable criteria that materially affect employment opportunities

TECH-LABS-0004.title: Evaluate for bias
TECH-LABS-0004.stmt: AI systems used in employment must be evaluated and mitigated for bias and may not result in discrimination based on protected characteristics or their proxies

TECH-LABS-0005.title: Right to explanation
TECH-LABS-0005.stmt: Applicants and employees have the right to receive meaningful explanations of AI-influenced employment decisions

TECH-LABS-0006.title: Ban intrusive monitoring
TECH-LABS-0006.stmt: Ban use of AI systems for continuous intrusive monitoring of workers including biometric tracking keystroke logging and real-time behavioral surveillance except where strictly necessary and proportionate

TECH-LABS-0007.title: No emotion inference
TECH-LABS-0007.stmt: Ban use of AI systems to infer or monitor worker emotions mood engagement or psychological state for employment decisions

TECH-LABS-0008.title: No outside-hours monitoring
TECH-LABS-0008.stmt: Employers may not use AI systems to monitor or infer worker behavior outside of working hours

TECH-LABS-0009.title: No sole-basis discipline or termination
TECH-LABS-0009.stmt: AI systems may not be used as the sole basis for disciplinary actions termination or compensation changes

TECH-LABS-0010.title: Transparent productivity scoring
TECH-LABS-0010.stmt: AI-generated productivity or performance scores must be transparent auditable and may not be used without human review

TECH-LABS-0011.title: No coercive productivity targets
TECH-LABS-0011.stmt: AI systems may not be used to coerce workers into unsafe unreasonable or exploitative productivity targets

TECH-LABS-0012.title: Right to refuse harmful AI
TECH-LABS-0012.stmt: Workers must have the right to refuse use of AI systems that materially harm their dignity privacy or safety without retaliation

TECH-LABS-0013.title: Access to human managers
TECH-LABS-0013.stmt: Workers must have access to human managers or decision-makers for matters affecting employment status discipline or working conditions

TECH-LABS-0014.title: Data minimization
TECH-LABS-0014.stmt: AI systems may only collect worker data that is strictly necessary for legitimate business purposes

TECH-LABS-0015.title: No data sales or repurposing
TECH-LABS-0015.stmt: Worker data collected through AI systems may not be sold shared or used for unrelated purposes

TECH-LABS-0016.title: Right to access and correct data
TECH-LABS-0016.stmt: Workers have the right to access review and correct data collected about them by AI systems

TECH-LABS-0017.title: Disclosure requirement
TECH-LABS-0017.stmt: Employers must disclose use of AI systems in hiring monitoring evaluation and decision-making processes

TECH-LABS-0018.title: Regular independent audits
TECH-LABS-0018.stmt: AI systems used in employment must be subject to regular independent audits for bias fairness and compliance

TECH-LABS-0019.title: Documentation requirement
TECH-LABS-0019.stmt: Employers must maintain documentation of AI system design purpose data sources and decision logic sufficient for oversight and accountability

TECH-LABS-0020.title: Ban behavior prediction scoring
TECH-LABS-0020.stmt: Ban AI systems that assign risk scores predicting employee behavior such as likelihood of quitting unionizing or engaging in protected activity

TECH-LABS-0021.title: Ban anti-union AI
TECH-LABS-0021.stmt: Ban use of AI systems to identify monitor or suppress union activity collective bargaining or labor organizing

TECH-LABS-0022.title: Validate personality assessments
TECH-LABS-0022.stmt: Ban use of AI systems that infer personality traits or psychological characteristics for hiring decisions without strong scientific validation and safeguards

TECH-LABS-0023.title: Worker role in AI deployment
TECH-LABS-0023.stmt: Workers or their representatives must have a role in reviewing and approving deployment of high-impact AI systems in the workplace

TECH-LABS-0024.title: Collective bargaining over AI
TECH-LABS-0024.stmt: Use of AI systems affecting working conditions must be subject to collective bargaining where applicable

TECH-MHCS-0001.title: AI assists not replaces clinicians
TECH-MHCS-0001.stmt: AI mental-health tools may assist but must not replace qualified clinicians in diagnosis crisis evaluation or high-risk treatment decisions

TECH-MHCS-0002.title: Prohibit manipulative AI systems
TECH-MHCS-0002.stmt: AI systems designed to induce emotional dependency, simulate coercive parasocial attachment, or exploit psychological vulnerabilities for commercial or political ends are prohibited

TECH-MHCS-0003.title: Evaluate for mental health harms
TECH-MHCS-0003.stmt: Platforms and AI systems must be evaluated for mental-health harms including addiction compulsive use isolation and self-harm amplification

TECH-MHCS-0004.title: Stronger protections for minors
TECH-MHCS-0004.stmt: Children and adolescents require stronger protections against manipulative AI systems and engagement optimization

TECH-MHCS-0005.title: Disclosure in sensitive contexts
TECH-MHCS-0005.stmt: Users must be clearly informed when they are interacting with an AI system in emotionally sensitive contexts

TECH-MILS-0001.title: Meaningful human control required
TECH-MILS-0001.stmt: Use of AI in military and intelligence contexts must preserve meaningful human control accountability and compliance with constitutional and international law

TECH-MILS-0002.title: Ban autonomous lethal targeting
TECH-MILS-0002.stmt: Ban AI systems that can independently select and engage targets with lethal force without meaningful human decision-making and accountability

TECH-MILS-0003.title: Ban AI force initiation
TECH-MILS-0003.stmt: Ban deployment of AI systems that can initiate or escalate use of force without real-time human authorization

TECH-MILS-0004.title: Ban nuclear AI
TECH-MILS-0004.stmt: Ban use of AI systems in nuclear command control targeting or launch decision processes

TECH-MILS-0006.title: No target filtering or ranking
TECH-MILS-0006.stmt: AI systems may not be used to narrow, filter, or rank potential human targets for lethal action in a manner that materially influences human decision-making

TECH-MILS-0007.title: Identified human decision-maker
TECH-MILS-0007.stmt: All use of lethal force involving AI systems must include a clearly identified human decision-maker responsible for final authorization

TECH-MILS-0008.title: Logging and auditability
TECH-MILS-0008.stmt: All AI-assisted military decisions must be logged and auditable including data inputs model outputs and human decision points

TECH-MILS-0009.title: Transparency for human oversight
TECH-MILS-0009.stmt: AI systems used in military decision-making must provide sufficient transparency to allow meaningful human understanding and oversight

TECH-MILS-0010.title: Testing and reliability
TECH-MILS-0010.stmt: AI systems used in military contexts must meet strict reliability and testing thresholds before deployment

TECH-MILS-0011.title: Ban mass intelligence fusion
TECH-MILS-0011.stmt: Ban large-scale AI-driven intelligence surveillance systems that indiscriminately collect or analyze civilian data without legal authorization and oversight

TECH-MILS-0012.title: No profiling for targeting
TECH-MILS-0012.stmt: Ban use of AI to profile individuals or populations for targeting based on probabilistic or behavioral inference alone

TECH-MILS-0013.title: Compliance with laws of armed conflict
TECH-MILS-0013.stmt: All AI military systems must comply with principles of distinction proportionality and necessity under the laws of armed conflict

TECH-MILS-0014.title: Clear attribution of responsibility
TECH-MILS-0014.stmt: Responsibility for actions taken using AI systems must be clearly attributable to human actors within a defined chain of command

TECH-MILS-0015.title: No evasion of accountability
TECH-MILS-0015.stmt: Use of AI systems must not be used to obscure responsibility or evade legal accountability for military actions

TECH-MILS-0016.title: Congressional authorization required
TECH-MILS-0016.stmt: Deployment of new classes of AI-enabled military capability must require explicit congressional authorization

TECH-MILS-0017.title: Limits on executive authority
TECH-MILS-0017.stmt: Executive authority may not unilaterally expand the use of AI in warfare beyond clearly defined and authorized limits

TECH-MILS-0018.title: Regular reporting to Congress
TECH-MILS-0018.stmt: Regular reporting to Congress and oversight bodies is required for all AI-enabled military programs

TECH-MILS-0019.title: Controlled testing required
TECH-MILS-0019.stmt: AI systems intended for military use must undergo controlled testing and evaluation prior to operational deployment

TECH-MILS-0020.title: No real-world testing
TECH-MILS-0020.stmt: Ban use of real-world military operations as primary testing environments for unproven AI systems

TECH-MILS-0021.title: Adversarial testing
TECH-MILS-0021.stmt: Require adversarial testing and red-teaming of AI systems to identify failure modes misuse risks and unintended escalation scenarios

TECH-MILS-0022.title: Contractor accountability
TECH-MILS-0022.stmt: Private contractors developing AI for military use are subject to the same legal and ethical constraints as government entities

TECH-MILS-0023.title: No outsourcing of authority
TECH-MILS-0023.stmt: Government may not outsource decision-making authority or accountability for use of force to private entities or AI systems

TECH-MILS-0024.title: Export controls
TECH-MILS-0024.stmt: Establish controls on export of high-risk military AI systems to prevent misuse by hostile actors or regimes with poor human-rights records

TECH-MILS-0025.title: Defensive AI permitted
TECH-MILS-0025.stmt: AI systems may be used in defensive or non-person-targeting contexts including missile defense threat detection and interception where the system operates against incoming projectiles vehicles or clearly defined non-human targets

TECH-MILS-0026.title: No repurposing defensive AI
TECH-MILS-0026.stmt: Defensive AI systems must not be repurposed or extended to identify select or target individual persons or groups

TECH-MILS-0027.title: AI for analysis not targeting
TECH-MILS-0027.stmt: AI may be used for analysis classification and situational awareness provided it does not generate or recommend specific human targets for lethal action

TECH-MILS-0028.title: Ban offensive lethal AI
TECH-MILS-0028.stmt: Ban use of AI systems in the execution of offensive lethal force including target selection engagement decisions and weapon deployment

TECH-MILS-0029.title: Limited guidance use
TECH-MILS-0029.stmt: AI systems may be used in limited weapon guidance roles solely to improve accuracy and reduce collateral damage after a human has independently selected and authorized a lawful target

TECH-MILS-0030.title: Guidance parameters strictly defined
TECH-MILS-0030.stmt: Guidance systems must not alter expand reinterpret or substitute the human-selected target and must operate within strictly defined engagement parameters

TECH-MILS-0031.title: No new target selection by guidance
TECH-MILS-0031.stmt: Guidance systems must be incapable of selecting new targets reprioritizing targets or initiating additional engagements

TECH-MILS-0032.title: Human responsibility for guidance outcomes
TECH-MILS-0032.stmt: Human operators remain fully responsible for all outcomes of AI-assisted weapon guidance including unintended harm or collateral damage

TECH-MILS-0033.title: Defensive systems against non-human threats
TECH-MILS-0033.stmt: AI may be used in defensive systems including missile interception threat detection and protection against incoming attacks where the system operates against non-human targets or active threats

TECH-MILS-0034.title: Support international treaties
TECH-MILS-0034.stmt: Promote and pursue international treaties to limit and regulate the use of AI in military and intelligence contexts

TECH-MILS-0035.title: Treaty goal: ban autonomous weapons
TECH-MILS-0035.stmt: International agreements should seek to prohibit fully autonomous lethal weapons and ensure meaningful human control over use of force

TECH-MILS-0036.title: Treaty goal: ban targeting AI
TECH-MILS-0036.stmt: International treaties should establish bans on AI systems that independently select and engage human targets

TECH-MILS-0037.title: Treaty goal: ban nuclear AI
TECH-MILS-0037.stmt: International treaties should restrict AI use in nuclear command control and strategic weapons systems

TECH-MILS-0038.title: Treaty mechanisms
TECH-MILS-0038.stmt: Treaties should include transparency reporting requirements and verification mechanisms for high-risk military AI systems

TECH-MILS-0039.title: Prevent AI arms races
TECH-MILS-0039.stmt: International efforts should aim to prevent destabilizing AI arms races that increase risk of rapid escalation or accidental conflict

TECH-MILS-0040.title: Export controls in treaties
TECH-MILS-0040.stmt: Support international controls on export and proliferation of high-risk military AI technologies to prevent misuse and human rights abuses

TECH-MILS-0041.title: Strong sanctions for violations
TECH-MILS-0041.stmt: Violations of international military-AI treaty obligations by adversaries should trigger strong sanctions and other enforcement measures

TECH-MILS-0042.title: Sanctions for prohibited systems
TECH-MILS-0042.stmt: Sanctions for deployment or export of prohibited military AI systems should be severe and coordinated internationally where possible

TECH-MILS-0043.title: Narrow reciprocal exceptions
TECH-MILS-0043.stmt: If an adversary deploys prohibited military AI systems, limited reciprocal emergency exceptions may be considered only under narrowly defined conditions and independent oversight

TECH-MILS-0044.title: Temporary reciprocal measures
TECH-MILS-0044.stmt: Any reciprocal exception to military AI prohibitions must be temporary, publicly reported to the fullest extent compatible with security, and subject to sunset and reauthorization

TECH-MILS-0045.title: Core prohibitions remain
TECH-MILS-0045.stmt: Core prohibitions on nuclear AI control, fully autonomous lethal targeting of persons, and unaccountable use of force remain in effect even during adversary noncompliance

TECH-MILS-0046.title: Autonomous targeting as war crime
TECH-MILS-0046.stmt: Use of AI systems to autonomously select or engage human targets or to materially remove human accountability in lethal force decisions constitutes a violation of the laws of armed conflict and should be treated as a war crime

TECH-MILS-0047.title: Target generation as unlawful
TECH-MILS-0047.stmt: Deployment of AI systems that generate or recommend human targets for lethal action or that substitute for human judgment in use-of-force decisions should be prohibited under international law and treated as unlawful conduct

TECH-MILS-0048.title: Violations of armed conflict principles
TECH-MILS-0048.stmt: Use of AI systems in ways that violate principles of distinction proportionality or necessity under the laws of armed conflict constitutes a war crime regardless of whether a human operator is nominally involved

TECH-MILS-0049.title: Precision guidance exception
TECH-MILS-0049.stmt: Use of AI solely for precision guidance to reduce collateral damage after lawful human target selection does not constitute prohibited offensive use provided it does not alter or expand the selected target

TECH-MILS-0050.title: Commanders remain liable
TECH-MILS-0050.stmt: Individuals and commanders remain legally responsible for unlawful outcomes resulting from use of AI systems and may not use AI as a defense against liability

TECH-MILS-0051.title: Dedicated cyber branch
TECH-MILS-0051.stmt: Establish a dedicated and fully developed cyber branch within the military focused on defensive and strategic cyber operations

TECH-MILS-0052.title: Cyber defense mission
TECH-MILS-0052.stmt: The cyber branch shall be responsible for defense of critical infrastructure military systems and national cyber assets against hostile cyber activity

TECH-MILS-0053.title: Offensive cyber limits
TECH-MILS-0053.stmt: Offensive cyber operations must be strictly limited authorized at the highest levels and subject to the same legal and oversight frameworks governing use of force

TECH-MILS-0054.title: Minimize civilian cyber impact
TECH-MILS-0054.stmt: Cyber operations must minimize impact on civilian infrastructure including healthcare utilities financial systems and communications networks

TECH-MILS-0055.title: Cyber branch oversight
TECH-MILS-0055.stmt: All cyber military operations are subject to oversight audit and accountability mechanisms equivalent to other military branches

TECH-MILS-0056.title: No domestic cyber surveillance
TECH-MILS-0056.stmt: The cyber military branch may not be used for domestic surveillance law enforcement or civilian monitoring except under narrowly defined and legally authorized emergency conditions

TECH-MILS-0057.title: Coordination with civilian agencies
TECH-MILS-0057.stmt: The cyber branch must coordinate with civilian agencies and private infrastructure operators while maintaining clear legal boundaries and accountability

TECH-MILS-0058.title: International cyber norms
TECH-MILS-0058.stmt: The cyber branch should operate in alignment with international efforts to establish norms and treaties governing cyber warfare and infrastructure protection

TECH-OVRG-0001.title: Public AI surveillance registry
TECH-OVRG-0001.stmt: Require public registration and disclosure of all government AI surveillance systems including purpose authority data sources and oversight structure

TECH-OVRG-0002.title: Regular independent audits
TECH-OVRG-0002.stmt: Require regular independent audits of all authorized government AI surveillance systems for legality bias accuracy and civil-liberties impacts

TECH-OVRG-0003.title: Sunset and reauthorization
TECH-OVRG-0003.stmt: All government AI surveillance authorities and systems must have sunset dates and require periodic reauthorization

TECH-PRIV-0001.title: Right to access without identification
TECH-PRIV-0001.stmt: Individuals have the right to access lawful online content and services without mandatory identity verification

TECH-PRIV-0002.title: Protect anonymous use
TECH-PRIV-0002.stmt: Anonymous and pseudonymous use of the internet must be protected as a component of free expression and privacy

TECH-SURS-0002.title: Ban AI mass public surveillance
TECH-SURS-0002.stmt: Ban AI-powered mass surveillance of public spaces except under narrowly defined high-threshold emergency conditions

TECH-SURS-0003.title: Ban persistent location tracking
TECH-SURS-0003.stmt: Ban persistent location tracking or cross-platform identity stitching by government without individualized judicial authorization

TECH-SURS-0004.title: Ban government data purchases
TECH-SURS-0004.stmt: Government may not purchase commercially collected surveillance data to evade constitutional limits

TECH-SURS-0005.title: Strict warrant requirements
TECH-SURS-0005.stmt: Require strict warrants minimization procedures and audit logs for surveillance access

TECH-SURS-0006.title: Ban predictive policing
TECH-SURS-0006.stmt: Ban predictive policing systems that rely on opaque risk scoring or historically biased enforcement data

TECH-SURS-0007.title: Ban suspicion scoring
TECH-SURS-0007.stmt: Ban suspicion scoring systems for public use by government agencies

TECH-SURS-0008.title: Automatic expiration
TECH-SURS-0008.stmt: Surveillance authorities must expire automatically unless affirmatively renewed under transparent review

TECH-SURS-0009.title: Ban network mapping
TECH-SURS-0009.stmt: Ban AI systems that map political social religious or associational networks of individuals absent individualized probable cause and judicial approval

TECH-SURS-0010.title: Protect journalists and attorneys
TECH-SURS-0010.stmt: Ban AI-enabled surveillance of journalists sources attorneys and protected communications absent the highest level of judicial review under narrowly defined circumstances

TECH-SYNS-0001.title: Ban deceptive synthetic media
TECH-SYNS-0001.stmt: Synthetic media must not be used to deceive the public about real people or events in ways that cause harm, manipulate democratic processes, or undermine individual rights

TECH-SYNS-0002.title: Ban synthetic impersonation
TECH-SYNS-0002.stmt: Ban the use of AI-generated media to impersonate a real person for fraud, financial gain, or unauthorized access

TECH-SYNS-0003.title: Ban identity verification bypass
TECH-SYNS-0003.stmt: Ban use of synthetic media to bypass identity verification systems

TECH-SYNS-0004.title: Ban non-consensual sexual content
TECH-SYNS-0004.stmt: Ban creation and distribution of non-consensual synthetic sexual content depicting real individuals

TECH-SYNS-0005.title: Ban false harmful depiction
TECH-SYNS-0005.stmt: Ban synthetic media that falsely depicts a real person saying or doing something in a way that would reasonably be understood as fact and would harm their reputation or safety

TECH-SYNS-0006.title: Ban political manipulation
TECH-SYNS-0006.stmt: Ban use of synthetic media in political advertising or messaging that depicts real individuals engaging in fabricated actions or speech

TECH-SYNS-0007.title: Ban misleading about officials
TECH-SYNS-0007.stmt: Ban synthetic media used to materially mislead the public about the health, actions, or status of public officials

TECH-SYNS-0008.title: Ban coordinated deception
TECH-SYNS-0008.stmt: Ban coordinated use of synthetic media to mislead the public about real-world events, elections, or public safety

TECH-SYNS-0009.title: Disclosure requirement
TECH-SYNS-0009.stmt: Require clear disclosure when media is substantially AI-generated or altered to depict realistic human activity

TECH-SYNS-0010.title: Provenance markers required
TECH-SYNS-0010.stmt: Require AI-generated video and audio to include persistent, tamper-resistant provenance markers

TECH-SYNS-0011.title: Open-source detection
TECH-SYNS-0011.stmt: Provenance markers must be detectable using publicly available, open-source, and free tools

TECH-SYNS-0012.title: Safeguards against removal
TECH-SYNS-0012.stmt: Developers of generative media systems must implement reasonable safeguards to prevent removal or circumvention of provenance markers

TECH-SYNS-0013.title: Allow parody and satire
TECH-SYNS-0013.stmt: Allow synthetic media for parody, satire, or artistic expression where it is clearly labeled or would not reasonably be interpreted as factual

TECH-SYNS-0014.title: Allow disclosed journalism use
TECH-SYNS-0014.stmt: Allow use of synthetic media in journalism or documentary contexts when clearly disclosed and not used to mislead about real events

TECH-SYNS-0015.title: Allow consensual use
TECH-SYNS-0015.stmt: Allow use of synthetic media depicting real individuals with explicit informed consent

TECH-HARS-0001.title: Platforms must implement effective systems to prevent and respond to targeted harassment campaigns
TECH-HARS-0001.stmt: Digital platforms with significant user bases must implement effective, consistently enforced systems to detect, prevent, and remediate targeted harassment campaigns, coordinated abuse, non-consensual intimate image distribution, and sustained hate-based attacks against individuals, with particular attention to harms disproportionately experienced by women, people of color, LGBTQ+ individuals, and other marginalized communities.

TECH-HARS-0002.title: Platform algorithmic systems may not amplify, recommend, or distribute harassing or hate-based content at scale
TECH-HARS-0002.stmt: Platform algorithmic recommendation, amplification, and distribution systems must not surface, promote, or spread harassing, threatening, or hate-based content, and must be subject to regular independent audits to detect and correct amplification of such content, with findings published in a format accessible to researchers and the public.

TECH-HARS-0003.title: Victims of coordinated online harassment have enforceable rights to platform response and escalated review
TECH-HARS-0003.stmt: Individuals subject to coordinated harassment campaigns must have access to expedited platform review, meaningful escalation procedures, effective tools to limit contact from abusers, and documented enforcement of platform rules against abusers, with platforms bearing accountability for systematic non-enforcement against documented coordinated campaigns.

TECH-PUBL-0002.title: AI systems used in core government services must use auditable, non-proprietary systems wherever feasible
TECH-PUBL-0002.stmt: AI systems deployed by federal agencies in core government services — benefits administration, regulatory enforcement, public health, law enforcement, immigration — must use open-source or otherwise independently auditable systems wherever technically and operationally feasible, and must not create long-term proprietary vendor lock-in that prevents independent audit, replacement, or public oversight.

TECH-PUBL-0003.title: Productivity gains from AI deployment in publicly funded services must be shared with workers and the public
TECH-PUBL-0003.stmt: Where AI deployment in publicly funded services, public-private partnerships, or critical infrastructure creates significant productivity gains, those gains must be measured, reported, and distributed — through wage increases, expanded service capacity, reduced costs to users, or return to the public fisc — rather than captured exclusively as private corporate profit while public workers are displaced and service quality stagnates.

TECH-MKTS-0001.title: Shared algorithmic systems used to coordinate prices across competing entities are prohibited as per se antitrust violations
TECH-MKTS-0001.stmt: The use of shared algorithmic systems, common pricing software, or AI-driven pricing models that coordinate prices or suppress competition among nominally competing entities in housing, labor markets, consumer goods, or services is prohibited as a per se antitrust violation, regardless of whether explicit communication occurred between competitors, when the algorithm's design uses competitor data or recommendations as inputs to set prices.

TECH-MKTS-0002.title: AI pricing and wage-setting systems in markets affecting housing, employment, or essential goods must be subject to antitrust review
